metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign explicit ownership (to a child or as an operator-executed step at merge time) for recording the three-part AC-2 measurement-framework artifact as a comment on the epic, covering (a) baseline, (b) re-runnable queries for both code-review and plan-review metrics, and (c) the link to 72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d.
findings.finding: Parent AC-2 — the in-session measurement-framework artifact (a) pre-change baseline recorded as epic comment, (b) exact re-runnable queries/commands for code-review block/false-block counts AND plan-review advisory-uptake, (c) link to the deferred follow-up ticket — has no child that owns producing this composite artifact. e1f2 partially mentions recording an observation plan, and 9f25 produces adjudication artifacts, but no child is scoped to assemble and record the three-part AC-2 artifact as a comment on the epic.
findings.location: Parent Acceptance Criteria — AC-2; no single child
findings.suggested_fix: Add an AC line to one child (e.g., e1f2 or b9c0, whichever closes last) requiring it to post the three-part measurement-framework comment on the epic, or document this as an operator-executed step in the epic's own ACs with [operator-attested] so the completion verifier can pass on attestation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Assign ownership of AC-3 (filing/confirming ticket 72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d with correct links and status) to a specific child or document it as an [operator-attested] step with a mechanism the completion verifier can confirm.
findings.finding: Parent AC-3 — the deferred post-deployment measurement follow-up ticket (72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d) must be filed, linked discovered_from this epic, and parked in idea with a data-collection criterion marked [operator-attested] — is owned by no child in the sibling roster.
findings.location: Parent Acceptance Criteria — AC-3; no child in roster
findings.suggested_fix: Either add an AC line to one child (e.g., the last-to-close child) asserting it confirms the follow-up ticket exists with the correct discovered_from link and idea status, or add [operator-attested] to AC-3 in the parent so the completion verifier can pass on operator attestation rather than requiring a child artifact.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify AC3's follow-up ticket: either add a direct link if the ticket already exists, or state 'create a new ticket and record its system-assigned ID as the canonical reference here' so the executor knows which action to take.
findings.finding: AC3 identifies the follow-up ticket by a UUID-like ID (72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d) but does not state whether this ticket already exists (in which case the executor verifies and links it) or must be created (in which case the system may auto-assign a different ID, invalidating the reference). An executor cannot determine which action to take without asking.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC3: 'filed as a separate follow-up ticket (72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d, linked discovered_from this epic, parked in idea)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace '(72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d)' with either a direct URL to the pre-existing ticket, or change the AC to read: 'filed as a separate follow-up ticket (record system-assigned ID here at merge time), linked discovered_from this epic, parked in idea.'
Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

findings.finding: b9c0's Acceptance Criterion 4 ('A replay of the post-recal corpus shows #518-class and #539-class findings now BLOCK') is structurally contingent on 9f25 deriving a threshold at or below 0.54 (for #518) and 0.60 (for #539). If 9f25's precision analysis yields a threshold above 0.54, the #518-class findings (impact 0.54) would not block, causing b9c0's own AC4 to fail — but b9c0 has no fallback or conditional framing for this scenario.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace b9c0 AC4 with: '- [ ] A replay of the post-recal corpus shows that findings with impact >= the derived block_threshold now BLOCK; the replay explicitly documents whether #518-class (impact 0.54) and #539-class (impact 0.60) findings fall above or below the threshold, consistent with 9f25's derivation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit ordering note (e.g., 'claim after ac0e is merged' or a depends_on) between e1f2 and ac0e, or narrow each child's file scope to non-overlapping sections and document the coordination contract.
findings.finding: Children ac0e and e1f2 both modify CLAUDE.md and docs/plan-review-gate.md without a stated ordering dependency or coordination mechanism. ac0e adds the no-signature-demands rule to CLAUDE.md's ticket-template section and docs/plan-review-gate.md; e1f2 removes the mandatory-remediation mandate from CLAUDE.md lines 112-121 and sweeps 'remediate' + 'advisory' across docs/. Concurrent or uncoordinated work on these shared files risks merge conflicts or one child's edits silently overwriting the other's.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a note to e1f2's Scope section that it should be claimed/merged after ac0e, OR add ac0e as a depends_on of e1f2, to ensure docs/plan-review-gate.md edits are sequential and e1f2's grep sweep runs against the already-updated file.

findings.finding: Parent AC2 (the in-session measurement-framework artifact recorded as an epic comment — baseline numbers, re-runnable queries, and a link to the deferred follow-up ticket) is not owned as an acceptance criterion by any child. e1f2's What prose mentions recording the observation plan on the epic, but its AC checklist does not include this as a verifiable item, so the completion verifier cannot confirm it was done.
findings.location: Parent AC2; no child AC covers it — e1f2 What prose gestures at it but e1f2's AC checklist omits it
findings.suggested_fix: Add to e1f2's AC checklist: '- [ ] A comment is posted on this epic recording (a) the 2026-07-12 baseline (0 finding-blocks / 162 changes; ~13 advisories/round), (b) the exact re-runnable queries for code-review block/false-block counts (REVIEW_RESULT code_review_result_v1 sidecars) and plan-review advisory-uptake (norm_id survival across rounds), and (c) the link to the deferred follow-up ticket 72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d.' Alternatively, create a dedicated child task scoped to writing the epic measurement-framework comment.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add to Success Criterion 4 (or child d4cf's scope) the specific artifact/mechanism that makes fire-rate observable — e.g., a named sidecar field, log key, or query — so the criterion is evaluable without subjective judgment.
findings.finding: Success Criterion 4 states the parent-containment criterion will be 'shipped advisory per the ADR-0036 expand-contract pattern with fire-rate observable from day one,' but does not specify what artifact or mechanism makes the fire-rate observable — no log field, sidecar key, dashboard, or query is named. An executor cannot confirm this criterion is met in-session without knowing what 'observable' means here.
findings.location: Success Criteria — item 4: 'fire-rate observable from day one (child d4cf)'
findings.suggested_fix: Append to SC4: 'observable via the plan-review sidecar's `norm_id` field (or equivalent criterion-level log key) queryable from day one of deployment,' matching the specificity used for the code-review measurement in AC2(b).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit rollback/kill-switch plan for the blocking threshold (e.g., a config flag or documented revert procedure) so the serious-tier block can be disabled quickly if post-deployment false-block rates are unacceptable.
findings.finding: The plan enables code-review blocking for serious-tier criteria in decide.py but specifies no explicit rollback or kill-switch mechanism if the enabled threshold produces an unacceptable false-block rate in production. The ADR-0036 A/B gate is a pre-flight go/no-go check, not a back-out mechanism once deployed.
findings.location: Definitions & Sequencing / Success Criteria (Code-review blocking bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Add a subsection under b9c0's deliverables: a named config toggle (e.g., `BLOCK_ENABLED_CRITERIA = [...]` in a settings file) that can be set to empty to revert to advisory-only without a code deploy, plus a sentence in the AC stating the toggle exists and is tested.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify where G7's reviewer-facing documentation lives (e.g., a rubric guide entry, an inline criterion description, or an ADR-0036 amendment) so human reviewers have narrative guidance on what constitutes a scope-containment violation.
findings.finding: The new G7 leaf-plan/parent-plan containment criterion (child d4cf) is a new contract for every plan reviewer, but the plan does not specify where or how this criterion is documented for human reviewers — only that it is 'shipped advisory per the ADR-0036 expand-contract pattern.' If G7 lives only in the rubric code file, reviewers lack narrative guidance on what 'scope containment' means, what evidence satisfies it, and how to call a violation.
findings.location: Success Criteria (bullet 4) / Context paragraph on d4cf

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify in the plan (or delegate explicitly to b9c0) whether the serious-tier block threshold is stored as a named, cohort-keyed constant (e.g. `BLOCK_THRESHOLD_CODE_V2`) with a comment linking to the ADR-0036 A/B gate requirement, so future re-calibration edits are unambiguous and the v2/v3 boundary is enforced structurally.
findings.finding: The blocking threshold in `decide.py` (code-v2 serious-tier criteria) is described as 'derived and pinned in a test' by child b9c0, but the plan does not specify whether the threshold value is hardcoded as a source constant or configurable externally — and since ADR-0036 anticipates re-calibration as the cohort grows, the mechanism for updating it without a risky in-place edit is unspecified.
findings.location: Definitions & Sequencing — 'b9c0 enables a block threshold … pins it in a test'; Success Criteria 1 — 'derived from adjudicated field data … passes the ADR-0036 A/B gate'
findings.suggested_fix: In b9c0's design, store the threshold as `BLOCK_THRESHOLD_BY_COHORT = {'code-v2': <derived_value>}` (keyed by `IMPACT_MODEL_VERSION`) so re-calibration for a new cohort adds a key rather than overwriting a scalar, and the pinned test checks the cohort key rather than a bare float.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add the leaf-plan parent-containment criterion (G7/d4cf) to the epic title or add a clearly labeled 'Also includes' line in the header so the full scope is visible without reading the body.
findings.finding: The title omits the new G7 leaf-plan parent-containment criterion (child d4cf), which is a non-trivial new plan-review capability added to this epic. A reader scanning only the title would have no indication this scope exists, creating a mismatch between the headline and the body's actual work.
findings.suggested_fix: Extend the title to: 'Review-gate calibration 2026-07-12: code-review blocking lane + advisory-guidance loosening + completion-verifier fixes + leaf-plan parent-containment criterion (G7)'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC that directly verifies the advisory-loosening artifact state — e.g., 'CLAUDE.md (or equivalent guidance file) no longer contains imperative language mandating remediation of all valid advisories, confirmed by a before/after diff linked in the merge comment.'
findings.finding: The advisory-guidance loosening goal ('Agent guidance no longer MANDATES advisory remediation') has no directly observable acceptance criterion — the only AC is 'all child tickets closed,' which is a pure proxy. There is no AC that specifies which artifact changes (e.g., a specific CLAUDE.md section), how a verifier confirms the mandate is removed, or what the before/after state looks like at the artifact level.
findings.location: Success Criteria (2nd bullet) / Acceptance Criteria (1st bullet)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: '[ ] The guidance artifact diff (CLAUDE.md or equivalent) is linked in the epic merge comment, showing the removal of mandatory-remediation language; a reviewer confirms no conflicting imperative remains in scope.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a positive rationale for the completion-verifier FAIL-persistence mechanism to the epic (or the responsible child): state whether FAILs will be persisted as a new event type (and whether it enters KNOWN_EVENT_TYPES / the reducer), as a structured comment, or via another path — so the architectural choice is explicit and its blast-radius on the event-sourced store is acknowledged.
findings.finding: The completion-verifier FAIL-persistence fix is a non-trivial design choice — currently FAILs are surfaced only as a raised CommandError with no durable store write (confirmed in transition_close.py lines 211-227); the plan's success criterion says 'FAILs are durably persisted' but the epic body states no mechanism for HOW (new event type? extended signing path? a structured store comment?) — leaving the approach's positive rationale absent for the most architecturally novel sub-problem in the epic.
findings.location: Success Criteria bullet 3: 'Completion-verifier FAILs are durably persisted and enumerate all unmet criteria in one verdict'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the rollback / back-out path for the new FAIL-persistence schema addition, including the asserted schema STATE after rollback (not just that rollback exits 0), and confirm no partially-written FAIL records can leave the verifier in a silently-passing state on partial failure.
findings.finding: The completion-verifier FAIL-persistence change adds a new durable-storage schema (currently 'FAIL verdicts persist nowhere'), but the plan specifies no rollback / back-out path for that schema addition. If the new persistence layer is deployed and then rolled back, the plan is silent on whether the schema is cleanly removed and whether any partially-written FAIL records could leave the verifier in an inconsistent state.
findings.location: Success Criteria — 'Completion-verifier FAILs are durably persisted' / Context paragraph on completion-verifier defects

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] In the Definitions & Sequencing 'serious-tier' definition, split the enumeration into two explicitly-labelled groups: (1) the consequence-binary fields that already score 0.9 in impact_code (which need a threshold derivation to become reachable) and (2) the `security` routing-criterion ID (which needs a `blocking_enabled` flip in criteria_routing.json) — and state the distinct mechanical change each group requires.
findings.finding: The 'serious-tier' definition conflates two structurally different activation mechanisms — consequence-binary fields (processed by impact_code's _CODE_PROD_BINARIES/_CODE_MAINT_BINARIES) and a routing-criterion ID (`security` in criteria_routing.json, processed by registry.threshold_for) — without specifying the decision rule that maps each type to a blocking_enabled flip. An executor implementing b9c0 must determine whether 'security' means flipping criteria_routing.json's `security.blocking_enabled: true` (a routing-registry change) while the five named binaries drive a separate threshold derivation, or whether all six are treated as co-equal routing-criterion flips. The `security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere` binary is already in _CODE_PROD_BINARIES at _CODE_TIER_SERIOUS — so a finding with that binary already scores 0.9 impact and would block at any derived threshold ≤0.9 WITHOUT a routing flip — but the plan lists it alongside `security` (the routing criterion) as if they require the same change.
findings.suggested_fix: Add two sub-bullets under the serious-tier definition: '**Consequence binaries (impact already 0.9, need threshold derivation + routing flip for the owning criterion ID):** data_loss_without_recovery, security_bypass_not_enforced_elsewhere, silent_wrong_feeding_a_decision, unversioned_published_contract_break, safety_net_removal_without_replacement — child b9c0 maps each binary to its owning routing criterion and flips blocking_enabled. **Routing criterion flipped directly (no binary mapping needed):** security.' This co-locates the decision rule with the schema it governs.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either name the child ticket (or create one) that delivers SC3's 'durably persisted FAIL verdict' and 'signed-children AC anti-pattern documented' deliverables, or explicitly note that SC3 is delivered inline within one of the named children (b9c0/9f25/f32e/d4cf) and identify which one and which AC covers it.
findings.finding: Success Criterion 3 ('Completion-verifier FAILs are durably persisted and enumerate all unmet criteria in one verdict; the signed-children AC anti-pattern is documented') names a concrete system-behavior deliverable but identifies no child ticket responsible for it and specifies no storage mechanism, persistence location, or fallback when the write fails. The four child tickets named or discoverable from the plan are b9c0 (blocking lane), 9f25 (threshold derivation), f32e (code-v3 binary), and d4cf (G7 criterion) — none covers the completion-verifier persistence fix. Currently verify_completion raises CommandError on FAIL (src/rebar/llm/completion.py) with no persistence path.
findings.location: Success Criteria — criterion 3; Acceptance Criteria — AC1 ('All child tickets closed')

findings.finding: Child ticket d4cf-0780-cbed-4879 ('Plan-review criterion: leaf-plan parent-containment') appears in the session log's ticket graph but is silently absent from the plan — not mentioned in the body, success criteria, acceptance criteria, or definitions section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a fifth Success Criterion (or expand an existing one) that explicitly covers the measurement framework artifact and the deferred follow-up ticket, so AC2 and AC3 have a parent SC and are not orphaned.
findings.finding: AC2 (in-session measurement-framework artifact with baseline, queries, and deferred link) and AC3 (deferred follow-up ticket 72b6-b0d2-2f93-456d filed) are concrete deliverables specified in the Acceptance Criteria section that have no covering Success Criterion. The four Success Criteria describe outcomes (blocking enabled, guidance loosened, verifier fixed, containment criterion shipped) but none names the measurement infrastructure or deferred tracking ticket as a success condition. This leaves two ACs orphaned — a verifier checking only the SCs would miss them, and a verifier checking only the ACs has no SC to anchor them to.
findings.location: Success Criteria (four bullet points) vs Acceptance Criteria items 2 and 3
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Success Criteria: 'A measurement framework is in place: an in-session artifact records the pre-change baseline and re-runnable post-change queries; multi-day field telemetry is deferred and tracked via a filed follow-up ticket (operator-attested).' This gives AC2 and AC3 a covering criterion.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
